35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  \uses{thm:quot_representable, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
76	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, def:has_abel_map,
77	        def:div_functor_carrier, def:pic_sharp_carrier}
78	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
79	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
80	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
81	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
82	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
83	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
84	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
85	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
86	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
87	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
88	  %   \end{thm}"
89	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
90	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
91	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
92	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
93	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
94	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
95	  %  of its associated sheaves."
96	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
97	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
98	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
99	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
100	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
101	  \[
102	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
103	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
104	  \]
105	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
106	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the
107	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
108	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
109	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
110	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
111	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
112	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
113	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
117	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
118	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
119	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
120	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
121	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
122	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
123	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
124	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
125	% [\dots]"
126	\begin{proof}
127	  
128	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
129	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
130	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
131	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
132	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
133	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
134	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
135	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
136	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
137	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
138	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
139	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
140	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
141	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
142	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
143	
144	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
145	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
146	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
147	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
148	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
149	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
150	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
151	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
152	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
153	  Composing with the canonical projection
154	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
155	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
156	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
157	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
158	\end{proof}
159	
160	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
161	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
162	
163	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
164	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
165	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
166	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
167	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
168	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
169	
170	\begin{theorem}\leanok
171	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
172	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
173	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
174	  \uses{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence, lem:smooth_proper_quotient,
175	        thm:quot_representable, def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
176	        cor:flattening_stratification_curve, def:pic_sharp_representable}
177	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
178	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X\) complete);
179	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
180	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
181	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
182	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
183	  %   Assume \(f\:X\to S\) is projective Zariski locally over \(S\), and is flat
184	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
185	  %
186	  %  \tu{(1)} Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, is separated and locally of finite
187	  %  type over \(S\), and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).
188	  %
189	  %  \tu{(2)} If also \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective, then
190	  %  \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is a disjoint union of open
191	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
192	  %  \(S\)-schemes.
193	  %   \end{thm}"
194	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
195	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
196	  %    Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(X\) is complete.  Then
197	  %  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists, represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\), and is a disjoint
198	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
199	  %   \end{sbscor}"
200	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
201	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
202	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
203	  over \(k\). Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
204	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
205	  representable by a \(k\)-scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), separated and locally of
206	  finite type over \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open
207	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
208	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is called the
209	  \emph{Picard scheme} of \(C/k\).
210	\end{theorem}
211	
212	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
213	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
214	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
215	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
216	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on \(S\) to
217	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of \(S\)-schemes.  Moreover, it
218	% is also a local matter on \(S\) to prove that an \(S\)-scheme is separated
219	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
220	% \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective.
221	% [\dots]
222	% Plainly an \(S\)-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
223	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
224	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
225	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
226	\begin{proof}
227	  
228	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
229	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
230	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
231	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
232	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
233	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
234	
235	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
236	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let $P^\phi \subseteq
237	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\( be the \'etale subsheaf whose \)T$-points are
238	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
239	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
240	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
241	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
242	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
243	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
244	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
245	  \(k\)-schemes.
246	
247	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
248	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
249	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
250	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
251	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
252	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
253	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
254	  \(k\)-scheme.
255	
256	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
257	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
258	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
259	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
260	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
261	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
262	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
263	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
264	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
265	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
266	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
267	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
268	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
269	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
270	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
271	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
272	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
273	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
274	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
275	  smooth and proper.
276	
277	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
278	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
279	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
280	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
281	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
282	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
283	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
284	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
285	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
286	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
287	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
288	  \(X = C\) complete case.
289	\end{proof}

291	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
292	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
293	
294	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
295	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
296	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the

299	\begin{lemma}\leanok
300	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
301	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
303	  \uses{def:has_smooth_proper_quotient}
304	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
305	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
306	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
307	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
308	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
309	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
310	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
311	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
312	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
313	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
314	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
315	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
316	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
317	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
318	  \begin{enumerate}
319	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
320	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
321	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
322	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
323	      and proper map.
324	  \end{enumerate}
325	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
326	  is representable by a smooth map.
327	\end{lemma}
328	
329	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
330	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
331	% structural heart of step 4.)
332	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
333	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
334	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
335	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
336	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
337	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
338	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
339	%
340	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
341	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
342	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
343	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
344	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
345	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
346	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
347	% [\dots]
348	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
349	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
350	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
351	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
352	%
353	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
354	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
355	% [\dots]
356	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
357	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
358	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~REF below, which is an
359	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
360	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
361	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
362	% represents \(P\)."
363	\begin{proof}
364	
365	\leanok
366	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
367	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
368	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
369	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
370	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
371	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
372	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
373	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
374	  closed embedding.
375	
376	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
377	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
378	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
379	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
380	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
381	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
382	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
383	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
384	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
385	
386	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
387	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
388	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
389	
390	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
391	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
392	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
393	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
394	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
395	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
396	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
397	\end{proof}

399	\section{Group-scheme structure}
400	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
401	
402	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
403	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
404	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
405	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
406	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
407	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
408	
409	\begin{theorem}\leanok
410	[\(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]
411	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
412	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
413	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
414	        def:pic_scheme_group_object}
415	
416	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
417	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
418	  % text (group-scheme structure on \(\IPic_{X/S}\) via tensor product);
419	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
420	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
421	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
422	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
423	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
424	  %      \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
425	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
426	  %  topologies  by
427	  %      \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
428	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
429	  %   \end{dfn}"
430	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
431	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
432	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
433	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
434	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
435	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
436	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
437	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
438	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
439	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
440	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
441	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
442	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
443	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
444	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
445	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
446	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
447	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
448	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
449	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
450	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
451	  scheme locally of finite type.
452	\end{theorem}
453	
454	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
455	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
456	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
457	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
458	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
459	% records the standard transfer.
460	\begin{proof}
461	  
462	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
463	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
464	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
465	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
466	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
467	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
468	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
469	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
470	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
471	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
472	  in the value category).
473	
474	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
475	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
476	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
477	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
478	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
479	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
480	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
481	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
482	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
483	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
484	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
485	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
486	\end{proof}

64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

168	## Rules
169

171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.

175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).

Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.

181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300	
301	## Logging
302	
303	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).

126	\begin{proof}
127	  
128	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
129	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
130	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
131	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
132	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
133	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
134	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
135	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
136	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
137	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
138	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
139	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
140	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
141	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
142	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
143	
144	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
145	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
146	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
147	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
148	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
149	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
150	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
151	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
152	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
153	  Composing with the canonical projection
154	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
155	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
156	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
157	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
158	\end{proof}
